title: Ring stacking rules for a clean, high-end look
excerpt: How to wear multiple rings without looking busy — spacing, finger selection, and the anchor principle.

sections.heading: Choose your hand
sections.body: Most people look better stacking on one hand only. Two rings per hand at most, concentrated on one side, reads more intentional than spreading rings across both hands randomly. Pick your dominant aesthetic hand and stack there.

sections.heading: The anchor principle
sections.body: Every ring stack needs one dominant piece. The anchor ring is the statement — typically heavier, with a more prominent face. The Obsidian Signet Ring works as a natural anchor because of its clean flat face and substantial band weight. Secondary rings should be thinner and simpler.

sections.heading: Finger placement
sections.body: Ring placement changes the entire read of the stack. A signet on the pinky reads classic and refined. The same ring on the index finger reads bold and directional. Middle finger is neutral — it is a good anchor position. Avoid wearing statement rings on adjacent fingers; let each piece breathe.

sections.heading: Match the finish across the stack
sections.body: Consistency in finish is more important in ring stacking than in chain stacking because rings are closer together and the eye compares them directly. If your anchor is black PVD, keep all additional rings in the same finish family. Two finishes at most — and only if there is a deliberate reason.

sections.heading: When three rings work
sections.body: Three rings can work on one hand: one on the index, one on the ring finger, and the anchor on the middle or pinky. This creates an asymmetric, editorial spread that photographs well and feels considered. The key is ensuring they are not all the same weight — vary the band thickness to create visual rhythm.

cta.heading: Start the stack with the Obsidian Signet Ring.
cta.body: Clean face, PVD finish, and a comfortable interior curve. Designed to anchor a stack without overshadowing it.
